title: Hashicorp Vault service tokens should not be disclosed
description: In most cases, trust boundaries are violated when a secret is exposed in a source code repository or an uncontrolled deployment environment. Unintended people who don't need to know the secret might get access to it. They might then be able to use it to gain unwanted access to associated services or resources.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.message: HashiCorp Vault Service Token detected (hvs. prefix). Vault service tokens grant access to Vault's secrets engine, PKI infrastructure, database credentials, and other managed secrets. Exposing this token allows attackers to retrieve all secrets the token has access to, potentially including database passwords, TLS certificates, cloud credentials, and application secrets managed by Vault. Revoke immediately via 'vault token revoke' and audit Vault audit logs.

HashiCorp Vault Legacy Service Token detected (s. prefix). Legacy Vault service tokens (s. prefix) provide authenticated access to Vault's secrets management APIs. Exposing this token allows unauthorized retrieval of secrets, credentials, and certificates managed by your Vault instance. Revoke immediately using 'vault token revoke' and audit all Vault access logs for unauthorized activity.

HashiCorp Vault token detected in variable assignment. Vault tokens are the primary authentication mechanism for HashiCorp Vault and control access to all secrets, encryption services, and PKI operations within your Vault instance. Root tokens have unrestricted access to the entire Vault. Revoke immediately via the Vault API or CLI and rotate all potentially exposed secrets.
